turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court in original action 156, New York versus New Jersey.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Under Article I Section 10 of the Constitution, each state possesses the Sovereign authority to enter into a compact with another state subject to Congress' approval. The compact is in essence a contract between two states. In 1953, New York and New Jersey exercised that authority and entered into the Waterfront Commission Compact, which was approved by Congress and signed by President Eisenhower. The compact created a bi-state agency of New York and New Jersey officials to perform certain regulatory and law enforcement functions at the port of New York and New Jersey to combat labor corruption and crime at the port, for example. In 2018, after concluding that the decades-old compact had outlived its usefulness, New Jersey sought to withdraw from the compact, but New York opposes New Jersey's withdrawal and contends that the compact does not allow either state to unilaterally withdraw. New York filed an action against New Jersey directly in this Court under this Court's original jurisdiction under Article III of the Constitution to entertain suits by one state against another state. The question presented is straightforward. Does the Waterfront Commission Compact allow New Jersey to unilaterally withdraw from the compact notwithstanding New York's opposition? We conclude that the answer is yes, that New Jersey May unilaterally withdraw from the compact. Some interstate compacts expressly allow, prohibit, or limit unilateral withdrawal, but this compact does not address withdrawal. Because the compact's text does not address whether a state may unilaterally withdrawal, we must look to background principles of law that would have informed the state's understanding when they entered the compact. This Court has long explained that interstate compacts are construed as contracts under the principles of contract law. Under the default contract law, at the time of the compact's 1953 formation as well as today, a contract like this compact that contemplates continuing performance for an indefinite time can be terminated at will by either party. So it is here. The Waterfront Commission Compact contemplates the commission's exercise of authority on an ongoing and indefinite basis. Therefore, the default contract law rule, namely the contract's calling for ongoing and indefinite performance may be terminated by either party, means that New Jersey May unilaterally withdraw from the compact. Principles of state sovereignty likewise support New Jersey's position. The background principle that a state does not easily seed its sovereignty informs our interpretation of this interstate compact. To be clear, the contract rule that we apply today governs compacts like this compact that are silent as to unilateral withdrawal and that exclusively call for ongoing performance on an indefinite basis, but that rule does not apply to other kinds of compacts that do not exclusively call for ongoing performance on an indefinite basis, such as compact setting land or water boundaries, apportioning water rights, or otherwise conveying property interests. In sum, the Court today holds the New Jersey may unilaterally withdraw from the Waterfront Commission Compact notwithstanding New York's opposition. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
